CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is overall well organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and its main contributions are stated early and clearly (single-layer random-filter models and multi-scale variants). Notation appears consistent and implementation/hyperparameter details are reported, and figures/tables/examples are informative; writing quality is described as good. Minor presentation issues remain—e.g., code release is not mentioned and some limitations of the VGG-based loss could be illustrated more concretely—preventing an exceptional rating. | B(4): The paper is well-organized and follows a logical flow from problem motivation through method, experiments, and conclusions, with the main contributions (single-layer random-filter synthesis and multi-scale extension) stated early. Notation appears consistent and hyperparameters are reported, and figures/examples (comparisons to Gatys et al. and Portilla & Simoncelli) are effective and informative for a reader concerned with perceptual quality. Writing quality is generally good with minimal ambiguity, though discussion of the VGG-based loss limitations and the computational inefficiency of the multi-scale model could be expanded for clarity. Overall presentation is strong with only minor areas where additional explanation would help non-expert readers.

Minor presentation issues remain—e.g., code release is not mentioned and some limitations of the VGG-based loss could be illustrated more concretely—preventing an exceptional rating.

Notation appears consistent and hyperparameters are reported, and figures/examples (comparisons to Gatys et al. and Portilla & Simoncelli) are effective and informative for a reader concerned with perceptual quality. Writing quality is generally good with minimal ambiguity, though discussion of the VGG-based loss limitations and the computational inefficiency of the multi-scale model could be expanded for clarity. Overall presentation is strong with only minor areas where additional explanation would help non-expert readers.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper presents a notable and somewhat surprising idea—that single-layer convolutional models with random, data-independent filters and Gram-matrix statistics can synthesize high-quality natural textures rivaling the multi-layer, pre-trained VGG approach of Gatys et al. This reframes assumed necessities (depth and supervised pretraining) and introduces a multi-scale single-layer variant; the authors clearly differentiate from Gatys et al. and Portilla & Simoncelli through controlled comparisons and a VGG-based quantitative loss. While the conceptual novelty is significant and would inform experts, it is an insightful simplification and extension of existing Gram-matrix-based texture synthesis rather than an entirely new problem formulation, so I rate it as notable but not revolutionary. | B(4): The paper delivers a notable and surprising perspective by showing that single-layer, random (data-independent) convolutional filters — especially in a multi-scale arrangement — can synthesize natural textures at perceptual quality comparable to the multi-layer, pretrained VGG Gram-based model (Gatys et al.). This directly challenges the assumed necessity of depth and supervised pretraining for high-quality parametric texture synthesis, a conceptual novelty beyond incremental tweaks. Although it reuses Gram-matrix statistics and optimization-based synthesis from prior work, the result that random single-layer banks suffice is a non-trivial and informative departure that would surprise practitioners familiar with deep feature-based texture models.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a notable and somewhat surprising idea—that single-layer convolutional models with random, data-independent filters and Gram-matrix statistics can synthesize high-quality natural textures rivaling the multi-layer, pre-trained VGG approach of Gatys et al. This reframes assumed necessities (depth and supervised pretraining) and introduces a multi-scale single-layer variant; the authors clearly differentiate from Gatys et al. and Portilla & Simoncelli through controlled comparisons and a VGG-based quantitative loss. While the conceptual novelty is significant and would inform experts, it is an insightful simplification and extension of existing Gram-matrix-based texture synthesis rather than an entirely new problem formulation, so I rate it as notable but not revolutionary.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper delivers a notable and surprising perspective by showing that single-layer, random (data-independent) convolutional filters — especially in a multi-scale arrangement — can synthesize natural textures at perceptual quality comparable to the multi-layer, pretrained VGG Gram-based model (Gatys et al.). This directly challenges the assumed necessity of depth and supervised pretraining for high-quality parametric texture synthesis, a conceptual novelty beyond incremental tweaks. Although it reuses Gram-matrix statistics and optimization-based synthesis from prior work, the result that random single-layer banks suffice is a non-trivial and informative departure that would surprise practitioners familiar with deep feature-based texture models.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The core empirical claim—that single-layer convolutional models with random or multi-scale filters can synthesize high-quality textures comparable to VGG-based multilayer models—is supported by experiments comparing to Gatys et al. and Portilla & Simoncelli and by a clear optimization procedure (Gram-matrix matching via L-BFGS-B). However, the evaluation relies heavily on a VGG-based loss (itself imperfect), lacks reported statistical variability (multiple seeds/replicates) and code release for reproducibility, and omits comprehensive ablations isolating contributions of filter type, scale, and initialization. These are important but relatively minor gaps: they do not obviously invalidate the main empirical findings, but they weaken confidence in generality and make replication harder. | B(4): The paper's primary experimental claim—that single-layer CNNs with random multi-scale filters can produce high-quality textures comparable to VGG-based multi-layer models—is supported by experiments comparing to Gatys et al. and Portilla & Simoncelli and by reported VGG-based reconstruction losses. Mathematical content appears straightforward and correct (no complex derivations), and experimental methodology uses relevant baselines; however, relying on a VGG-based Gram loss as an objective evaluation metric risks bias toward VGG-style representations and there is limited discussion of statistical testing or variability measures. Given these minor methodological concerns (and acknowledged computational limitations), the technical claims are largely supported but not entirely free of caveats.

reviewer_a.justification: The core empirical claim—that single-layer convolutional models with random or multi-scale filters can synthesize high-quality textures comparable to VGG-based multilayer models—is supported by experiments comparing to Gatys et al. and Portilla & Simoncelli and by a clear optimization procedure (Gram-matrix matching via L-BFGS-B). However, the evaluation relies heavily on a VGG-based loss (itself imperfect), lacks reported statistical variability (multiple seeds/replicates) and code release for reproducibility, and omits comprehensive ablations isolating contributions of filter type, scale, and initialization. These are important but relatively minor gaps: they do not obviously invalidate the main empirical findings, but they weaken confidence in generality and make replication harder.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's primary experimental claim—that single-layer CNNs with random multi-scale filters can produce high-quality textures comparable to VGG-based multi-layer models—is supported by experiments comparing to Gatys et al. and Portilla & Simoncelli and by reported VGG-based reconstruction losses. Mathematical content appears straightforward and correct (no complex derivations), and experimental methodology uses relevant baselines; however, relying on a VGG-based Gram loss as an objective evaluation metric risks bias toward VGG-style representations and there is limited discussion of statistical testing or variability measures. Given these minor methodological concerns (and acknowledged computational limitations), the technical claims are largely supported but not entirely free of caveats.

main_claims: A single-layer convolutional neural network with random, data-independent filters can synthesize natural textures of high perceptual quality, often rivaling the state-of-the-art multi-layer VGG-based method (Gatys et al., 2015a).
Depth of the network and supervised pre-training of feature maps are not necessary for high-quality parametric texture synthesis; multi-scale single-layer models particularly improve results.
Gram matrices of CNN feature maps provide useful summary statistics for texture synthesis, and a quantitative VGG-based loss can be used as an objective measure of texture quality.
_summary.methodology: Compute Gram matrices of feature activations from single-layer CNNs (various filter types and sizes, including random and multi-scale). Synthesize textures by optimizing an image from noise to match the reference Gram matrices using L-BFGS-B and report VGG-based reconstruction loss as the evaluation metric.
baselines: Gatys et al. (2015a) VGG-based texture model
limitations: Multi-scale single-layer model is computationally inefficient due to very large convolutional filters (up to 55×55).
The VGG-based loss is not a perfect perceptual metric and can fail for highly regular man-made structures (e.g., walls, grids).
Trade-off between perceptual similarity and variability is not solved; measuring variability (entropy) is intractable and robust variability measures are lacking.
For finite-size images, synthesis can in some cases exactly reconstruct the reference (over-constrained), indicating possible finite-size effects and reduced sample variability.
_summary.topic_area: Computer Vision - texture synthesis / image generation

reviewer_a.name: Rigorous Experimentalist
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML researcher / engineer with extensive experience in empirical evaluation of vision models and reproducible research; has published and reviewed many NeurIPS/ICLR papers on model evaluation and ablation.
traits: insists on comprehensive ablations to isolate the contribution of each component (filter type, scale, initialization, optimization hyperparameters)
values reproducibility: expects clear implementation details, code release, and precise optimization settings
looks for appropriate quantitative metrics and statistical reporting (e.g., multiple seeds, variance, failure cases)
notices potential confounders in experimental setups (dataset selection, pre/post-processing, evaluation bias)
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Focuses on the experimental rigor and clarity: will accept novel claims only if supported by controlled comparisons, reproducible procedures, and robust quantitative analyses.

reviewer_b.name: Texture & Generative Vision Researcher
reviewer_b.background: Associate professor working on image synthesis, texture modeling, and perceptual evaluation of generative models; familiar with classical texture synthesis, neural texture representations (Gatys et al.), and more recent generative frameworks.
traits: prioritizes conceptual novelty and how the work shifts understanding of what components are necessary in texture synthesis
attends to connections with prior art (statistical models of texture, filter banks, Gram-based representations) and whether the paper advances theoretical or practical insights
values perceptual quality and real-world impact (e.g., efficiency, scalability, controllability) over small numerical gains
notices broader implications for related tasks (style transfer, generative modeling) and potential follow-up directions
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Assesses novelty and significance in context, weighing whether the results change prevailing assumptions about necessary ingredients for high-quality texture synthesis and whether the contribution opens useful new directions.
